name: Debug and Profile Python Apps from the CLI
description: Use Python's built-in debugger (pdb), profiling tools (cProfile), and memory analysis to find bugs and performance bottlenecks from the command line.

steps.title: Run a script with the debugger
steps.description: Start the script under the Python debugger. Execution pauses at the first line, letting you step through code, set breakpoints, and inspect variables.
steps.tip: Use breakpoint() in your code to set inline breakpoints instead of running the entire script under pdb.

steps.title: Run with post-mortem debugging on crash
steps.description: If a script crashes, use pdb.pm() to open the debugger at the point of the exception. You can inspect the traceback, local variables, and call stack.
steps.warning: The simpler approach is to add 'breakpoint()' on the line before the crash and run normally, which drops you into pdb at that exact location.

steps.title: Profile a script with cProfile
steps.description: Run the script with the built-in profiler and sort results by cumulative time. This shows which functions consume the most total time including their callees.

steps.title: Save profiling data and analyze it
steps.command: python -m cProfile -o profile.prof script.py && python -m pstats profile.prof
steps.description: Save profiling data to a binary file and open the interactive pstats browser. You can sort, filter, and drill into call trees.
steps.tip: Use 'snakeviz profile.prof' for a visual flame graph in your browser. Install with pip install snakeviz.

steps.title: Measure memory usage with tracemalloc
steps.command: python -c "import tracemalloc; tracemalloc.start(); exec(open('script.py').read()); snapshot = tracemalloc.take_snapshot(); [print(s) for s in snapshot.statistics('lineno')[:10]]"
steps.description: Trace memory allocations and display the top 10 lines by memory consumption. This helps identify memory leaks and excessive allocations.

steps.title: Time a specific code snippet
steps.description: Benchmark a code snippet by running it 1000 times and reporting the average execution time. The -s flag runs setup code once before timing begins.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Debug and profiling workflow
set -e

# Create a sample script to debug/profile
cat > sample_script.py << 'PYEOF'
import time

def slow_function(n):
    """Deliberately slow function for profiling demo."""
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        total += sum(range(i))
    return total

def fast_function(n):
    """Optimized version using math."""
    return sum(i * (i - 1) // 2 for i in range(n))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    n = 500
    print(f"Running slow_function({n})...")
    result1 = slow_function(n)
    print(f"Result: {result1}")

    print(f"Running fast_function({n})...")
    result2 = fast_function(n)
    print(f"Result: {result2}")

    assert result1 == result2, "Results should match"
    print("Both functions produce the same result.")
PYEOF

echo "=== Profiling with cProfile ==="
python -m cProfile -s cumulative sample_script.py

echo ""
echo "=== Timing comparison ==="
python -m timeit -n 100 -s 'from sample_script import slow_function' 'slow_function(100)'
python -m timeit -n 100 -s 'from sample_script import fast_function' 'fast_function(100)'

echo ""
echo "Profiling complete. Review the output above to identify bottlenecks."

faq.question: What are the most useful pdb commands?
faq.answer: The essentials: n (next line), s (step into function), c (continue to next breakpoint), p variable (print value), l (list source), w (show call stack), q (quit). Use h for the full command list.

faq.question: How is cProfile different from profile?
faq.answer: cProfile is a C extension and has much lower overhead than the pure-Python profile module. Always use cProfile for real profiling. The profile module exists mainly for extending the profiler's functionality.

faq.question: What tools give a visual view of profiling data?
faq.answer: snakeviz renders cProfile data as an interactive flame graph in your browser. py-spy is a sampling profiler that can attach to running processes and generate flame graphs without modifying code. Both install via pip.
